executive_summary: Data pipeline mostly failed Saturday: production DB, GA4, Search Console, and Stripe were all down, so there are no order, revenue, or customer numbers. Google Ads was the only live source — it spent $141.90, the highest day in the last week.
analyst_brief:
**The story today is the pipeline, not the business.** Almost every data source failed overnight, so I can't tell you how many orders came in, what they were worth, or how many new customers we paid to acquire. The scorecard is blank on purpose — those are missing readings, not real zeros. Please don't read this as a bad day; read it as a blind day.

Here's what actually broke and why it matters:

- **Production database is down** with a clear, fixable cause: the error says `pymysql not installed`. That's a missing software package on the machine that runs the morning job, not a problem with the database itself. A one-line install (`pip3 install pymysql`) on the ThinkPad should bring orders, revenue, customers, ratings, cancellations, and failed payments back. **This is the one thing to fix today.** Without it, every number that decides priorities is gone.
- **Search Console and GA4 also failed** with a similar "module not found" / not-yet-integrated message, which suggests the same environment got wiped or rebuilt and lost its installed packages. Worth checking all of them together rather than one at a time.
- **Stripe and Intercom are down for a simpler reason** — no API keys are configured. That's a setup gap, not a crash.

**The only thing I can actually see is Google Ads, and it raises a flag.** We spent $141.90 yesterday — the most of any day in the last week (the daily average was about $106.82, and the full week ran $747.77). On its own that's not alarming, but here's the risk: the system kept spending ad money while we had no ability to measure what that money produced. The 84 clicks and 1,312 impressions are real, but the "4 conversions" Google reports are registrations, not paying orders — and with the database down, we can't confirm a single one turned into an order. So we have a day where money went out the door and we're flying blind on what came back. If the database stays down for several more days, I'd seriously consider holding ad spend flat or pausing the higher-spend campaigns until we can see results again — spending more while measuring nothing is the worst combination.

One small observation for the manual-review pile, not an action: the paid search terms yesterday were almost all generic "laundromat near me" / "coin laundry near me" types that didn't convert, while the one term that did convert was simply "laundry." Nothing to do about it today — just noting it so we can look properly once the data is trustworthy again.

Bottom line: get the database package reinstalled, re-run today's data gather, and confirm orders and revenue are flowing before trusting tomorrow's brief. Until then, hold ad spend steady rather than letting it climb further unmeasured.
validation_notes: No cross-check possible: production DB and Stripe were both down, so DB-vs-Stripe order reconciliation could not run. Search Console, GA4, ClickUp, and Intercom were also unavailable. Google Ads was the only source returning data. Treat today's scorecard as empty, not as zeros.
data_footnotes: Production DB: UNAVAILABLE — query failed with 'pymysql not installed'. No orders, revenue, AOV, customer-type, ratings, cancellations, or failed-payment data for 2026-06-20.
Computed metrics block was empty — no 7-day averages, percentage changes, or cost-per-new-customer values were available to populate the scorecard.

Google Ads: account 972-828-9560, only working source. Yesterday spend $141.90, 1,312 impressions, 84 clicks, 4 reported 'conversions' (registrations, not purchases — cannot verify as orders with DB down). 7-day spend $747.77, avg daily $106.82.
Search Console: UNAVAILABLE ('No module named google'). Indexing health for all 42 monitored pages could not be assessed.
Stripe: UNAVAILABLE (no API key) — DB-vs-Stripe order cross-check could not run.
ClickUp / Intercom: UNAVAILABLE (Phase 2 / no API key).
Revenue methodology (when data returns): all monetary values net of 13% HST, tips excluded.

Historical source mix is limited to TGE daily acquisition metrics. Live same-day source labels use best-effort GA4/BigQuery joins.

days.executive_summary: Solid Thursday. Delivered revenue hit $837.75 ex. HST (+32.1% vs 7d avg) on 13 orders with big baskets ($64.44 delivered AOV). Placed orders flat at 12. The sore spot: cost per new customer was $36.93 — well above the $25 ceiling — and the 7-day rolling figure ($47.46) is worse.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good revenue day. We delivered 13 orders worth $837.75 ex. HST, about a third above the recent norm, and the orders were unusually large ($64.44 delivered average). Placed orders sat right on the 7-day line at 12. Nothing broke, ratings are still excellent (4.69, one low rating), cancellations are near zero. So the day itself is fine.

The thing worth your attention is where the ad money went. We spent $110.78 and got 3 new customers, which works out to $36.93 each — half again over the $25 ceiling. And the 7-day rolling figure is worse at $47.46, so this isn't a one-day blip; we've been paying too much to acquire customers for a week.

The leak is concentrated in one place. The **"City Target (GTA) Click" campaign spent $62.70 — more than half the entire day's ad budget — and produced zero trackable signups.** Meanwhile small campaigns did the work: Vaughan ($6.49 → 3 registrations) and Calgary ($9.59 → 1) carried the day on pennies. The GTA Click campaign is the single biggest drag on your acquisition cost. I'd take a hard look at it. Note: the registration counts come from Google's tracking, which counts signups, not real orders, so treat them as a rough guide — but a campaign spending the most and showing nothing is a clear pattern, not noise.

On the search-term side, a lot of GTA Click money is flowing to "laundromat near me" variants that aren't converting. I'm flagging this as an observation only — do **not** add negative keywords off this; that needs your manual review (past negatives blocked good searches). Just worth knowing what the budget is buying.

One quieter worry: how many new customers come back. The April groups reordered at 28–41% within three weeks, but the May groups have dropped to single digits (6.7%, 8.3% for the last two weeks shown). The most recent weeks aren't fully mature yet — the 21-day window hasn't closed — so don't over-read it, but the direction is down and it's worth watching. Paying $37–47 to acquire someone only pays off if they come back, so the acquisition-cost problem and the reorder problem compound each other.

On search rankings: Toronto (position 20.9) and Ottawa (11.8) get lots of impressions but barely any clicks because they sit too deep to be seen. The city pages share one template for titles, so this isn't a one-page fix — the body copy is the only per-city lever. Lower priority than the ad spend question.

The one thing today: figure out what the GTA Click campaign is actually doing for $60+/day, because it's the main reason new customers cost more than they should.
